title: fab Intake
description: Structured input contract for the fab coordinator agent. Any agent or system that wants to trigger the fab startup team sends a message conforming to this schema as the first user event.

goal.description: What the team should accomplish. Be specific about the desired outcome, not the process.

workflow.description: Optional predefined workflow to follow. If omitted, the coordinator decides the sequencing. Workflows are tagged factory (build pipeline), firm (business), or lab (meta).

constraints.description: Boundaries the team must operate within.

budget.description: Resource budget signal — affects scope, tooling, and infrastructure choices.

language.description: Primary language for this build. Required for code-producing workflows (feature-build, mobile-ship, infra-setup, security-audit, perf-review, incident, automate, launch-prep). Dispatches the factory's LANGUAGE_TOOLCHAIN — build / lint / test / docs commands, manifest file, version-lookup command, registry. If the build spans languages, pick the primary and list the others in language_versions.

language_versions.description: Current stable versions for the language runtime and critical ecosystem components. Drafters declare versions explicitly — 'latest stable first' is the idiom. Example: {'node': '22', 'typescript': '5.9'} or {'python': '3.13', 'go': '1.24'}. EOL versions (Python ≤3.9, Node ≤18, Go ≤1.20) are rejected at intake.

context.description: Background the team needs to do their work. See docs/INTAKE_GUIDE.md for the quality bar on each field.

client.description: Client or customer name. Used for branding, tone, and project routing. Avoid placeholder names like 'Acme Corp' — pick a name with character even for fictional clients.

product.description: Product or feature name. Slugged into the branch name (`feat/<slug>`) and the project subdirectory (`protohype/<slug>/`).

problem.description: The core problem being solved, in the user's words. One paragraph. The team uses this to pick the right architecture and to write the PR description.

audience.description: Target users or personas. Influences UX, security model, and scaling assumptions.

existing_systems.description: Integrations the new build must respect. SSO platform (with features), source systems, data warehouse, observability stack, CRM, etc. Without this, the team guesses and usually picks a stack that doesn't connect to the customer's reality.

success_criteria.description: Falsifiable, measurable assertions. Each one must be answerable yes/no on a future date with a number or named test. '<3s p50 latency', '≥80% answers cite a source', 'zero leaks in red-team test'. Vague claims like 'fast' or 'reliable' are rejected by intake-analyst.

security_requirements.description: Named anti-patterns to avoid, drawn from the threat model and past failures. 'Identity via Okta users.info, never fabricated email', 'Audit writes are awaited, never fire-and-forget', 'Every external client has explicit per-call timeout', 'Bedrock inference logging set to NONE in CDK, not just commented'. The factory production-bar covers generics; this section specializes.

out_of_scope.description: Explicit scope fence with concrete examples. 'Voice and mobile SDK', 'Cross-workspace federation', 'Auto-submit to vendor — human approval required'. Required on tight timelines; protects from scope creep mid-workflow.

roles.description: Restrict which roles participate. If omitted, the coordinator engages all relevant roles. Roles are organized into factory (30), firm (29), and lab (5) teams.

artifacts.description: Specific deliverables to produce. If omitted, the coordinator decides based on the goal and workflow.

examples.goal: Ship Almanac — an internal Slack bot for NanoCorp that answers employee questions grounded in Notion, Confluence, and Google Drive. Every answer cites sources with page/doc URLs and last-modified timestamps, and results are filtered to what the asking user has access to in the source system — no data leaks across private spaces. Users invoke the bot as @almanac in Slack channels and DMs.

context.problem: Employees waste hours hunting across Notion, Confluence, and Google Drive. Tribal knowledge isn't searchable. Existing Slack search is keyword-only and doesn't cross tools.
context.audience: NanoCorp employees (engineering, sales, ops) asking questions in Slack DMs and team channels
existing_systems: Okta SSO (SAML + SCIM for workforce identity)
success_criteria: Every answer includes at least one cited source with URL + freshness timestamp
Zero cross-tenant / cross-space data leaks verified by red-team test
Stale-source warning surfaces when Almanac cites a doc >90 days old
security_requirements: Per-user identity propagation from Slack → Okta → source-system OAuth
Answers must respect source-system ACLs end-to-end; Almanac falls back to 'I don't have access' on redacted hits — not a TODO
Audit log of every Almanac query + retrieved doc IDs + user ID, retained 1 year, with retry and DLQ on transient failures
No source content stored in model provider logs — prompt caching with PII scrubbing
Rate limiter must use shared state (Redis or DynamoDB) — multi-instance deployment, no in-memory Maps
Per-user OAuth token storage must scale to 10k+ users cost-effectively — shared-secret + DynamoDB token store, not one Secrets Manager secret per user
